note.soap:
S:  
Patient is a 13-17 year old with a recent tick bite in Georgia, presenting with fever and chills. Tick may have been attached for 24-72 hours. No rash reported. Patient is not pregnant.

O:  
No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. No rash or other symptoms reported.

A:  
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Early tick-borne illness (possible early Lyme disease or other tick-borne infection) given tick exposure and fever.  
- Differential: Early Lyme disease, ehrlichiosis, anaplasmosis, viral illness.  
- Red flags considered: High fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, altered mental status, difficulty breathing, rash (none reported).

P:  
- Disposition: Urgent in-person evaluation recommended due to fever after tick bite and regional risk.  
- Plan: Advise patient/guardian to seek prompt in-person medical evaluation for physical exam, possible laboratory testing (CBC, blood smears, Lyme serology), and initiation of empiric treatment if indicated. Educate on signs of worsening illness.  
- Safety net: Return immediately if symptoms worsen, including high fever, severe headache, rash, difficulty breathing, or neurological symptoms.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
